channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: U.S. closes two gulf embassies as Iran widens strikes. Follow our live coverage.
item.description: The embassies in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait were shut after drone attacks as Iran’s Revolutionary Guard launched strikes around the region Tuesday. Israel carried out fresh attacks against high-profile targets in Iran and said it would deploy ground troops deeper into Lebanon, as Hezbollah said its militants targeted Israel with drones.

item.title: Iran conflict. Trump poll. Clinton depositions. Midterms. Supreme Court. ICE training. Test your strength. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: National Park Service database flags hundreds of items that might ‘disparage’ America
item.description: An internal government database reviewed by The Washington Post demonstrates the vast scope of the Trump administration’s ongoing effort to revise or remove information on African American history, climate change and other topics at hundreds of national park sites.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Longtime PTA partner rants about the ‘horrors of working with me’
item.description: The two put the PTA on their backs for 15 years. The letter writer just learned how the other really feels about working together.

item.title: We texted 1,000 Americans about U.S. strikes in Iran. Here’s what they said.
item.description: More Americans oppose the strikes than support them, a flash poll found. Perceptions of Trump’s goals vary widely, though a clear majority say his administration has not clearly explained them. Still, about half think the U.S. military’s actions will contribute to long-term U.S. security.

item.title: This person’s sartorial choices are the topic of today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Monday points.

item.title: The economy is changing. Don’t forget who fears it most.
item.description: Artificial intelligence doomer narratives reflect personal concerns, not economic reality.

item.title: U.S. expects more casualties, Joint Chiefs Chairman Dan Caine says, as Iran war widens. A 4th U.S. service member has died.
item.description: The U.S. military expects to take additional casualties in its operation against Iran, Caine said in a rare on-camera news briefing, adding that accomplishing objectives will take “some time" and “in some cases, will be difficult and gritty work.”

item.title: Iran attack. Democrats’ response. Texas primary. AI newspaper. Winter blast. Microplastics. Lunar eclipse. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: 3 U.S. jets downed in ‘apparent friendly fire’; Middle East conflict spreads to Lebanon. Follow our live coverage.
item.description: The fighter jets went down Sunday night over Kuwait, U.S. Central Command said, amid active combat in which all six crew members safely ejected. The Middle East conflict escalated as Iran’s salvos against Persian Gulf states continued and Hezbollah fired rockets and drones at Israel, prompting Israeli strikes on the group's strongholds in Lebanon.

item.title: Trump leaves room to claim victory — or avoid blame — in recorded Iran strike messages
item.description: President Donald Trump has taken an approach to selling U.S. citizens on military action in Iran that contrasts with his predecessors, seeking to avoid completely owning it.

item.title: Where Iran has retaliated across the Middle East, according to satellite images and videos
item.description: Iran intensified its retaliatory strikes against American allies in the Persian Gulf and Israel on Sunday, attacking sites in at least nine countries since the start of the joint U.S.-Israeli attack.

item.title: Man who killed 2 in Austin may have been motivated by attack on Iran, officials say
item.description: The gunman, who also wounded 14 others at a bar in Austin early Sunday before he was killed by officers at the scene, has been identified as a 53-year-old naturalized U.S. citizen who came to the country from Senegal in 2006, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: San Francisco Ballet cancels planned performances at the Kennedy Center
item.description: The ballet company joins a long list of groups and artists, including composer Philip Glass, soprano Renée Fleming and banjoist Béla Fleck, who have backed out of events at the cultural institution.

item.title: At last, the credibility of U.S. deterrence is being restored, George F. Will writes
item.description: The perhaps 30,000 protesters who perished in Iran’s streets in early January did not die in vain.

item.title: Trump’s attack on Iran risks alienating war-weary supporters
item.description: President Donald Trump’s major attack on Iran has rattled parts of the coalition that twice delivered him the White House, a fracture that could spell trouble for a divided GOP as the midterm elections approach. While the more hawkish wing heralded the opening salvo, others accused Trump of betraying the populist ideology that propelled him to power.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Boyfriend books a year of travel to his races and nixes other trips
item.description: Boyfriend’s ultramarathons take up all his vacation time – and, apparently, willingness to consider the letter writer’s feelings.

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Play now to finish out your score for the week.

item.title: 3 U.S. service members killed and 5 seriously wounded as part of Iran operation, Central Command says. Follow our live coverage.
item.description: In announcing the first reported U.S. casualties in the joint attack with Israel on Iran, the military also said several other service members suffered minor shrapnel injuries and concussions.

item.title: At least 9 dead in strike near Jerusalem, Israel says, as Iran retaliates across the region. Follow our live coverage.
item.description: Nearly 30 more were injured in the strike, according to Israel’s national emergency service. Attacks across the Middle East continued Sunday, with Israel saying it launched a fresh wave in “the heart of Tehran” and Iran striking in Israel and around the Persian Gulf.

item.title: How a push from Israel and Saudi Arabia helped move Trump to attack Iran
item.description: Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman made multiple private phone calls to President Donald Trump over the past month advocating a U.S. attack, despite his public support for a diplomatic solution, according to four people familiar with the matter. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, meanwhile, continued his long-running public campaign for U.S. strikes against what he views as an existential enemy of his country.

item.title: Satellite images and videos capture damage from strikes in Iran, including to supreme leader’s compound and a school for girls
item.description: The visuals provided the first window into where U.S. and Israeli strikes have landed across Iran, revealing targets that include the Tehran compound of the country’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed. The total number of casualties in Iran was not immediately clear, but satellite imagery and videos show damage to civilian areas.

item.title: Reza Pahlavi, Iranian democratic opposition leader and son of Iran’s last shah: The hour of freedom is at hand
item.description: The path forward is a new constitution followed by free elections under international oversight.

item.title: Trump says Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, has died, and adds that military operation will continue
item.description: President Donald Trump, who earlier said the operation was aimed at toppling the Iranian government, said in a Truth Social post that the military operation would continue “uninterrupted throughout the week or, as long as necessary to achieve our objective of PEACE THROUGHOUT THE MIDDLE EAST AND, INDEED, THE WORLD!”

item.title: Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed in strike, Israeli security officials say
item.description: Khamenei was killed in an Israeli airstrike on his Tehran compound, according to four Israeli security officials briefed on the matter, who spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive intelligence. U.S. officials said they did not immediately have confirmation of Khamanei’s death.

item.title: U.S. military says it hit Iran’s missile sites, air defense, command centers; official says spy agency strike induced panic
item.description: U.S. Central Command gave its first official statement since the strikes began early Saturday. Top commanders at Iran’s main intelligence agency were targeted by Western airstrikes on Saturday, inducing panic in the security agency’s ranks, a senior Western security official told The Washington Post, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss military operations.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Burned-out husband takes a week off work — and kids, and chores
item.description: Carolyn Hax: Burned-out husband takes a week off work – and kids, and chores

item.title: The rotten case for war with Iran
item.description: The Middle East is not as crucial as it was and the U.S. military is stretched thin.

item.title: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.
item.description: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.

item.title: Freedom for Iran is the goal of the ‘major military operation’ that began this morning, Trump tells The Post
item.description: The president spoke to The Washington Post early Saturday after announcing that the U.S. had begun strikes on Iran to bring about regime change.

item.title: Trump announces U.S. has launched ‘major combat operations in Iran’
item.description: “Our objective is to defend the American people by eliminating imminent threats from the Iranian regime,” the president said in a video address on social media. The announcement came after Israel said that it had carried out strikes on Tehran early Saturday.

item.title: Israel says it has launched strikes on Iran, as explosions heard in Tehran
item.description: Several explosions were reported in Tehran, according to Iranian media. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said that it had carried out a “preemptive strike against Iran” and that a state of emergency had been declared across the country.

item.title: Analysis: Ranking the 2028 Democratic presidential contenders
item.description: The possibilities include governors, senators, House members and even celebrities. And although no one has officially declared their candidacy yet, some are sending strong signals that they'll run.

item.title: Correction: Scouts say they will continue to allow trans children to join. An earlier alert said they would be denied entry under new rules.
item.description: In announcing the Pentagon’s agreement with Scouting America, the defense secretary assailed the group for welcoming transgender children but stopped short of saying they would be denied entry. A previous alert incorrectly said that the organization would ban transgender children. This alert has been corrected.

item.title: Neil Sedaka dies at 86. The singer and songwriter was a pop hitmaker in two eras.
item.description: He rose to stardom in the 1950s with hits like “Breaking Up Is Hard to Do,” then made a roaring comeback in the ’70s with “Laughter in the Rain” and “Love Will Keep Us Together.”

item.title: Trump orders U.S. government to drop use of Anthropic’s technology, in dispute over access to its AI
item.description: President Donald Trump made a dramatic intervention in the dispute between Anthropic and the Pentagon over the use of AI for autonomous weapons or surveillance.

item.title: Pete Hegseth strikes new deal with Scouts: Girls allowed for now, DEI and transgender children banned
item.description: The defense secretary said Scouting America made the concessions to preserve its long-standing relationship with the military but will remain under review.

item.title: Bill Clinton tells GOP-led panel he had no knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein’s crimes
item.description: The appearance in New York marks the first time a former president has been compelled to testify before Congress under a subpoena.

item.title: Carolyn Hax chat: My mom tries to ruin my birthday every year
item.description: Carolyn Hax chat: My mom tries to ruin my birthday every year

item.title: A new car, home feel out of reach for middle-class Americans, poll finds
item.description: Most renters — including those earning six figures — doubt they could afford a home in the foreseeable future.

item.title: The hypothetical nuclear attack that escalated the Pentagon’s showdown with Anthropic
item.description: Start-up Anthropic and the U.S. military are careening toward a clash over government use of artificial intelligence — and whether it should be allowed to kill.

item.title: 10 questions, and don't forget the bonus wager! Finish off your week with On the Record.
item.description: Think you’ve got what it takes to earn a perfect score? Take today’s longer news quiz and complete your point total for the week.

item.title: Election powers. Refugee death. Iran talks. Hillary Clinton. Anthropic showdown. Snow maps. Neanderthal mating. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: 'No chance' that military strikes against Iran would lead to drawn-out war, Vance says
item.description: In an interview with The Washington Post, Vance said he remains a “skeptic of foreign military interventions.”

item.title: Anthropic rejects Pentagon’s terms for lethal use of its chatbot Claude, setting up clash with Hegseth
item.description: CEO Dario Amodei said Anthropic could not permit its technology to be applied to domestic mass surveillance or fully autonomous weapons. The Pentagon gave Anthropic a 5:01 p.m. Friday deadline to comply or risk being forced to provide full access to its AI under the Defense Production Act.

item.title: Netflix won't raise bid for Warner Bros., clearing a path for Paramount to take over the studio
item.description: The streaming giant said it will not increase its bid to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery, potentially paving the way for Paramount Skydance to swoop in and buy the company after a tense war between two media mega-corporations.

item.title: DOJ reviewing whether Epstein files with Trump allegations were wrongly withheld
item.description: Three summaries of interviews the FBI conducted in 2019 with a woman who had accused Donald Trump of sexually assaulting her are missing from the files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, multiple news outlets have reported. The woman had accused Trump of sexually assaulting her decades earlier while she was a minor. No evidence has emerged publicly to corroborate that accusation.

item.title: Neanderthal males and human females had babies together, ancient DNA reveals
item.description: Scientists at the University of Pennsylvania examined Neanderthal DNA and modern human DNA to find evidence that couplings between these species followed the same parental pattern over interbreeding events separated by 200,000 years.

item.title: Trump’s White House ballroom project can continue for now, judge rules
item.description: The National Trust, which has sued to stop the ballroom, said it would immediately revise its complaint and try again.

item.title: Judge rules IRS broke federal law ‘approximately 42,695 times’ in giving data to DHS
item.description: The judge wrote that the vast majority of the nearly 47,300 taxpayer addresses the IRS shared with Immigration and Customs Enforcement in August were disclosed without the IRS confirming that ICE provided a valid address for the person whose records it was seeking.
